stem: Let \( f(x) = \begin{cases} [[a]]x + ([[b]]) & \text{if } x < [[t]] \\ [[c]]x + ([[d]]) & \text{if } x \ge [[t]] \end{cases} \). What is \( f([[x0]]) \)?
solution: Compare \( [[x0]] \) with the breakpoint \( [[t]] \) to pick the correct piece, then substitute. This gives \( f([[x0]]) = [[answer]] \).

This substitutes the breakpoint \( [[t]] \) into the lower piece instead of the input \( [[x0]] \).

stem: Let \( f(x) = \begin{cases} [[a]]x + ([[b]]) & \text{if } x < [[t]] \\ [[c]]x + ([[d]]) & \text{if } x \ge [[t]] \end{cases} \) and \( g(x) = [[m]]x + ([[n]]) \). What is \( f(g([[x0]])) \)?
solution: First evaluate the inner function: \( g([[x0]]) = [[m]]([[x0]]) + ([[n]]) \). Compare THIS value (not \( [[x0]] \) itself) with the breakpoint \( [[t]] \) to pick the correct piece of \( f \), then substitute. This gives \( f(g([[x0]])) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This applies \( f \)'s lower piece directly to \( [[x0]] \), forgetting to first evaluate \( g \) and compare its output (not \( [[x0]] \)) to the breakpoint.

This correctly substitutes \( g([[x0]]) \) into \( f \) but uses the wrong piece (compared \( [[x0]] \) rather than \( g([[x0]]) \) to the breakpoint).

stem: Let \( f(x) = \begin{cases} [[a]]x + ([[b]]) & \text{if } x < [[t]] \\ [[c]]x + ([[d]]) & \text{if } x \ge [[t]] \end{cases} \). Given that \( V = [[V]] \) is attained on the \( x \ge [[t]] \) piece, which of the following is a solution of \( f(x) = [[V]] \)?
solution: Since we are told \( V \) is attained on the \( x \ge [[t]] \) piece, solve that piece's equation: \( [[c]]x + ([[d]]) = [[V]] \), giving \( x = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the breakpoint itself instead of solving the branch equation.

This negates the correct value (a sign slip when isolating \( x \)).

stem: Let \( f(x) = \begin{cases} [[a]]x + ([[b]]) & \text{if } x \le [[t]] \\ [[c]]x + ([[d]]) & \text{if } x > [[t]] \end{cases} \). What is \( f([[t]]) \)?
solution: The breakpoint \( x = [[t]] \) satisfies \( x \le [[t]] \) (the inequality is not strict there), so the FIRST piece applies: \( f([[t]]) = [[a]]([[t]]) + ([[b]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the second piece, but the condition \( x \le [[t]] \) on the first piece includes the breakpoint itself.
